Name: rate_distortion_pruning
Title: Rate-Distortion Pruning: A Theory-Driven Approach to Model Compression
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured approach to pruning based on rate-distortion theory can yield quantifiable improvements in predictive sufficiency while minimizing task-relevant distortion, directly linking model complexity to information-theoretic limits.
Related Work: Recent works in model compression have explored dynamic precision allocation and geometric filtering (e.g., SQR, PCA filtering). However, these approaches often emphasize empirical performance without a solid theoretical foundation. Our approach distinguishes itself by deriving pruning strategies rooted in rate-distortion theory, aiming to provide reproducible bounds and explicit quantifications of predictive sufficiency.
Abstract: This proposal seeks to explore a novel framework for model compression through a rate-distortion lens, focusing on pruning strategies that minimize task-relevant distortion while maintaining predictive sufficiency. Leveraging principles from information theory, we will establish a quantifiable relationship between model complexity, rate-distortion bounds, and mutual information with the task. Our approach will involve deriving theoretical limits on the bits-per-weight necessary for effective pruning, informed by description length and mutual information measures. We will validate this framework through experiments involving structured pruning of neural networks, assessing the resulting trade-offs in model accuracy, complexity, and generalization. The anticipated outcome is a deeper understanding of the fundamental limits of model compression, providing a theoretical basis for pruning strategies that can be widely applied across various domains.
Experiments: 1. Develop a theoretical framework that quantifies the achievable rate-distortion trade-off for various neural network architectures, deriving bounds on bits-per-weight for predictive sufficiency.
2. Implement structured pruning algorithms that apply these theoretical insights, systematically varying the level of pruning and measuring the resultant model accuracy and distortion.

3. Conduct experiments on benchmark datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, MNIST) to evaluate the performance of our pruning strategies against existing methods, using metrics such as accuracy, model size, and task-relevant distortion.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The primary risk involves the complexity of accurately modeling the rate-distortion relationship for diverse architectures. Additionally, there may be challenges in translating theoretical insights into practical pruning algorithms. Limitations may also arise from the specific nature of the datasets used, potentially affecting generalization.
